summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / press freedom, AI / computing, AI / privacy. The strongest items in this run are Live updates from Elon Musk and Sam Altman’s court battle over the future of OpenAI, Whole Wheat Sourdough Pita (With Video), and Partial US government shutdown ends after Congress votes to fund DHS.

mainItems.title: Live updates from Elon Musk and Sam Altman’s court battle over the future of OpenAI
mainItems.summary: Sam Altman and Elon Musk are facing off in a high-stakes trial that could alter the future of OpenAI and its most well-known product, ChatGPT.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Touches trust, surveillance, and the private use of AI systems.

mainItems.title: Whole Wheat Sourdough Pita (With Video)
mainItems.summary: Last year, I co-taught a baking class for The Baker’s Corner members at Chimacum Valley Grainery in Washington with my good friend Graison Gill.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Lower-stakes, but high fit for the maker/craft side of your brain.

mainItems.title: Partial US government shutdown ends after Congress votes to fund DHS
mainItems.summary: Congress advances Homeland Security funding after Republicans used procedural tool to pass up to $75bn for ICE and border patrol Sign up for the Breaking News US email to get newsletter alerts in your inbox A historically long 75-day…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Microsoft’s DLSS competitor is now available on the Xbox Ally X handheld
mainItems.summary: Microsoft is starting to test its Automatic Super Resolution (Auto SR) feature with the Xbox Ally X. Auto SR first debuted on some Copilot Plus PCs nearly two years ago, improving visual quality and frame rates in select games.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: This startup’s new mechanistic interpretability tool lets you debug LLMs
mainItems.summary: The San Francisco–based startup Goodfire just released a new tool, called Silico, that lets researchers and engineers peer inside an AI model and adjust its parameters—the settings that determine a model’s behavior—during training.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: Now California’s cops can give tickets to driverless cars
mainItems.summary: Autonomous vehicles roving California's roads will no longer be immune to traffic tickets starting on July 1st.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: $100 Nintendo gift cards are $20 off at Newegg today
mainItems.summary: Nintendo eShop gift cards can be used to buy games, apparel, and even hardware or accessories from its online storefront.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: US court refuses to hear Trump’s appeal of E Jean Carroll $83m defamation case
mainItems.summary: Decision from federal appeals court comes almost 18 months after Trump appealed following 2024 election win Sign up for the Breaking News US email to get newsletter alerts in your inbox A federal appeals court has announced it will not…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Genius in the Glitch: Why CS Needs Mismatched Brains
mainItems.summary: Have you ever had that student, the one who can dream up a stunningly complex, creative solution to a problem... The post Genius in the Glitch: Why CS Needs Mismatched Brains appeared first on Computer Science Teachers Association .
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: Musk faces third day of questioning in contentious trial over OpenAI’s founding
mainItems.summary: Trial continues after heated back and forth during OpenAI’s cross-examination of the Tesla CEO on Wednesday Elon Musk’s court case against Sam Altman continues on Thursday, after a day of contentious exchanges during OpenAI’s…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Senate Education Committee Chair Bill Cassidy Fights to Keep His Seat
mainItems.summary: It only took about a minute for Sen. Bill Cassidy to get choked up earlier this month during a forum on dyslexia.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.title: People Are Selling Kills of Marathon’s Hardest Boss on eBay
mainItems.summary: The Compiler takes a serious amount of time, skill, and luck to get to. Someone on eBay is selling an easy fix.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: To catch colorectal cancer early, advocates push to make 'poop talk' OK
mainItems.summary: It's a very treatable form of cancer if caught early, yet younger adults rarely get screened. Patient advocates want more people to talk to their doctors about risk factors and number two.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

new.title: Constellation
new.why: A little more cerebral and mood-heavy, but it scratches the identity / reality-slippage itch.

new.title: The Devil’s Hour
new.why: Dark, twisty, and structurally ambitious without feeling like homework.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

older.title: Continuum
older.why: A cleaner old-school sci-fi fit if you want procedural momentum wrapped around time-travel politics.

older.title: Person of Interest
older.why: Starts procedural and then quietly becomes one of the smartest AI / surveillance shows on television.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.

highlights.title: Solve a murder mystery by flying people around on your giant crane in the Disco Elysium and Chinese folktale inspired The Crane Rider's Tale
highlights.notes: Pinned from today's briefing at James's request.

highlights.title: 12 unexpected sourdough bread recipes that aren't crusty loaves: From sourdough flatbread to sourdough milk bread — and even sourdough babka.
highlights.notes: Pinned from today's briefing, the kind of bread-nerd rabbit hole worth keeping.

highlights.title: A.I. Is Prompting an Evolution, Not Extinction, for Coders
highlights.notes: Interesting piece on the current state of AI in software work.

highlights.title: The Workers Letting A.I. Do Their Jobs
highlights.notes: In an era of agents powered by artificial intelligence, many programmers are barely programming.
